findings.summary: nodes.py (798 LOC) handles node CRUD, container lifecycle, workspace files, identity sync, network topology, recommendations, and child creation in one file.
evidence: 28 route handler functions across 7 distinct domain concerns in a single 798-line file
Network topology query (lines 574-631) has no relationship to workspace file CRUD (lines 397-484)
Recommendations parsing (lines 677-706) and child node creation (lines 727-797) are domain features unrelated to container lifecycle (lines 291-340)
The file has 21 disconnected function clusters per static analysis
findings.suggestion: Split into focused route modules: node_crud.py (create/read/update/delete), node_container.py (restart/rebuild/logs/stats), node_workspace.py (files read/write/list), node_network.py (topology/peer config), node_children.py (recommendations/child creation). Each module would be under 200 LOC with a clear single responsibility.

findings.summary: The pattern of reading identity.json, checking name drift, writing corrected identity back is duplicated verbatim across three separate code paths.
evidence: nodes.py get_node_identity (lines 556-565): reads identity, checks name != node.name, writes back corrected copy
chat.py _check_identity (lines 56-70): identical pattern with read, name drift check, JSON dumps, write_workspace_file
main.py _refresh_identities (lines 174-192): same pattern applied in maintenance loop
All three use the same json.loads -> name comparison -> json.dumps(indent=2) -> write_workspace_file sequence
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared async function like `sync_identity_name(container_id: str, expected_name: str, identity: dict) -> dict` in helpers.py that performs the drift check and write-back. All three call sites become one-liners.

findings.summary: The network topology query (parents/children/siblings via edges) is implemented independently in nodes.py and chat.py with near-identical logic.
evidence: nodes.py get_node_network (lines 574-631): queries edges_out, edges_in, sibling_ids, fetches all related nodes, builds summary dicts
chat.py _get_network_context (lines 104-142): same edge queries, same sibling computation, same node_summary calls
Both use identical SQL patterns: select(Edge).where(Edge.source_id == node_id) and Edge.target_id == node_id
Only difference is chat.py omits edge_type in node_summary calls
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `async def get_network_topology(node_id: UUID, db: AsyncSession) -> dict` in helpers.py that both endpoints call. The edge_type inclusion can be controlled by an optional parameter.

findings.summary: AgentLoop.__init__ accepts 14 parameters, many of which are related configuration values that should be grouped.
evidence: Lines 48-65: __init__(self, bus, provider, workspace, model, max_iterations, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window, brave_api_key, exec_config, cron_service, restrict_to_workspace, session_manager, mcp_servers, channels_config)
model, max_iterations, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window are all agent behavior params that already exist as AgentDefaults in config/schema.py
brave_api_key is a single tool config value threaded through the constructor
The from_config classmethod (lines 108-150) exists precisely because the constructor is too wide
findings.suggestion: The from_config classmethod is the right direction but the raw __init__ should accept a smaller surface. Group model/temperature/max_tokens/max_iterations/memory_window into a single AgentDefaults (or a new AgentParams dataclass) parameter, and pass tools config as a single ToolsConfig rather than extracting brave_api_key/exec_config/restrict_to_workspace individually.

findings.summary: CronService manually serializes/deserializes CronJob dataclasses to/from JSON dicts instead of using Pydantic or dataclass_json, creating a fragile repeated structural pattern.
evidence: Lines 84-113: _load_store manually unpacks each JSON field into CronJob, CronSchedule, CronPayload, CronJobState constructors with get() calls and camelCase-to-snake_case mapping
Lines 130-165: _save_store manually packs every field back into dicts with the reverse camelCase mapping
Both directions repeat every field name twice and any new field requires updating both methods
The project already uses Pydantic extensively (config/schema.py has 20+ Pydantic models with camelCase alias support)
findings.suggestion: Convert CronJob, CronSchedule, CronPayload, and CronJobState to Pydantic BaseModel subclasses (with the same camelCase alias generator used in config/schema.py). Replace _load_store/_save_store with CronStore.model_validate_json() / store.model_dump_json(by_alias=True, indent=2). This eliminates 80+ lines of manual mapping.

findings.summary: The chat_relay WebSocket handler contains 3-4 levels of nested closures and try/except blocks, making control flow hard to follow.
evidence: Lines 146-292: chat_relay is a single 146-line function with nested async def frontend_to_nanobot (line 171) and nanobot_to_frontend (line 201)
Inside nanobot_to_frontend, there are 4 levels: try -> async for -> try -> if/elif branches each with their own try/except + async with
The nonlocal frontend_connected flag (lines 169, 172, 202) creates implicit coupling between the closures
The task management logic (lines 263-280) adds another layer of complexity at the same indentation level
findings.suggestion: Extract frontend_to_nanobot and nanobot_to_frontend as module-level async functions that accept their dependencies (websocket, nanobot_ws, node_id, container_id, node_name) as parameters. Use an asyncio.Event for the frontend_connected signal instead of nonlocal. This flattens the nesting by one level and makes each function independently testable.

findings.summary: Both LLM providers catch all exceptions and return error messages as LLMResponse.content strings instead of raising, making API call failures indistinguishable from valid LLM responses.
evidence: litellm_provider.py lines 227-232: except Exception as e: return LLMResponse(content=f'Error calling LLM: {str(e)}', finish_reason='error')
custom_provider.py lines 32-33: except Exception as e: return LLMResponse(content=f'Error: {e}', finish_reason='error')
The caller in agent/loop.py _run_agent_loop (line 236) has no way to distinguish an LLM error from a valid response containing the word 'Error'
finish_reason='error' is set but never checked by any caller — the agent loop only checks response.has_tool_calls (line 244) and response.content (line 276)
findings.suggestion: Either raise a custom LLMError exception from the providers and catch it in the agent loop, or check finish_reason == 'error' in _run_agent_loop before proceeding. The current pattern means authentication failures, rate limits, and network errors silently become 'assistant' messages stored in session history.

findings.summary: All nanobot tools return errors as 'Error: ...' string prefixes instead of raising exceptions, but the registry also catches exceptions and converts them to the same format, creating a dual error path.
evidence: registry.py line 51: checks if isinstance(result, str) and result.startswith('Error') to append a hint — a fragile string-prefix convention
registry.py line 54: also catches Exception and returns 'Error executing {name}: ...' — so tools have two ways to report errors
filesystem.py: all 4 tool classes catch Exception and return f'Error: ...' strings (lines 65, 109, 171, 244)
paradise.py lines 77-79: returns error string for HTTPStatusError, but lines 78-79 return early on first sub-operation failure, skipping subsequent operations
web.py WebSearchTool line 99: returns f'Error: {e}' while WebFetchTool line 160: returns json.dumps({'error': str(e)}) — two different error formats in sibling tools
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one strategy. The recommended approach for LLM tool calls is to always return strings (since they go back to the LLM), but define a ToolError dataclass or use a Result type so the registry can detect errors without string-prefix matching. For example: return ToolResult(success=False, message='...') or raise ToolError('...') and let the registry format it consistently. Also unify the error format — WebFetchTool should not return JSON-encoded errors when all other tools return plain strings.

findings.summary: docker_ops.py functions use three different error strategies: return None, silently log and continue, or propagate exceptions to caller — with no clear rule for which strategy applies.
evidence: get_container_status (lines 56-65): returns 'not_found' or 'unknown' strings — never None, never raises
get_container_logs (lines 68-77): returns None on NotFound AND on APIError — caller cannot distinguish 'missing container' from 'API failure'
get_container_stats (lines 80-101): returns None on NotFound or KeyError — swallows KeyError which could mask bugs in stats parsing
write_nanobot_config (lines 144-158): silently logs and returns on failure — caller cannot know the write failed
write_workspace_file (lines 191-203): same silent pattern — failure is invisible to caller
run_container_command (lines 230-247): propagates NotFound and APIError directly — opposite of all sibling functions
stop_nanobot_container (lines 46-53): swallows NotFound (reasonable) but would propagate APIError (inconsistent with other functions)
findings.suggestion: Adopt a consistent pattern: functions that are informational (get_*) should return Optional types with None for any failure. Functions that perform mutations (write_*, create_*) should raise on failure so callers can decide how to handle it. run_container_command already follows the 'raise' pattern — extend it to write_nanobot_config and write_workspace_file, then let the route handlers decide whether to catch or propagate.

findings.summary: Backend route handlers use inconsistent strategies: some raise HTTPException on container errors, others silently catch and set node.container_status = 'error', hiding failures from the API caller.
evidence: create_node (lines 121-126): catches setup_container Exception, sets node.config = {'error': str(exc)}, returns 200 success — caller sees a 'created' node with error state buried in config
rebuild_node (lines 331-335): catches setup_container Exception, raises HTTPException 500 — caller gets a clear error
restart_node (lines 304-308): catches _recreate_container Exception, raises HTTPException 500 — consistent with rebuild
clone_node (lines 269-271): catches Exception, sets error state, returns 200 — same silent pattern as create_node
create_child_node (lines 763-767): catches Exception, sets error state, returns 200 — same silent pattern
Three operations silently succeed with error state vs two operations that properly fail — inconsistent caller experience
findings.suggestion: Choose one strategy for container creation failures. The cleanest approach: always raise HTTPException on failure (matching restart/rebuild), so callers get a clear 500 error. If the intent is to create the DB record even when the container fails, document this as an explicit design decision and return a distinct HTTP status (e.g., 207 Multi-Status or include a top-level 'warnings' field in the response).

findings.summary: nanobot/server.py uses print() for all output instead of the logging/loguru infrastructure used everywhere else in the codebase.
evidence: server.py line 64: print(f'[paradise] Created {item.name}', flush=True)
server.py line 111: print(f'[paradise] Agent initialized, model=...', flush=True)
server.py line 115: print(f'[paradise] Agent init failed: {exc}', flush=True)
server.py line 123: print(f'[paradise] Client connected: {peer}', flush=True)
server.py line 200: print(f'[paradise] Client disconnected: {peer}', flush=True)
Backend uses logging.getLogger (main.py, nodes.py, docker_ops.py, chat.py), nanobot-src uses loguru logger everywhere — server.py uses neither
findings.suggestion: Replace all print() calls in server.py with loguru's logger (matching the nanobot-src convention since server.py imports from nanobot.*). This ensures consistent log formatting, log levels, and output routing. Agent init failures should be logger.error, connections should be logger.info, etc.

findings.summary: Multiple channel implementations silently swallow exceptions with bare 'except Exception: pass' or 'except: pass', losing error context for debugging.
evidence: matrix.py has 4 'except handler silently suppresses error' instances and 1 'empty except block (except: pass)' per static analysis
qq.py has 1 'except handler silently suppresses error', 1 'empty except block', and 3 'catch block that only logs'
server.py lines 181-182: on_progress callback wraps websocket.send in try/except Exception: pass — progress delivery failures are completely invisible
The existing_findings data shows 14 files with 'Except handler silently suppresses error' or 'Catch block that only logs' — a systemic pattern across channels
findings.suggestion: At minimum, add logger.debug() to all bare except:pass blocks so that failures are traceable when debug logging is enabled. For the server.py on_progress callback, at least log at debug level so WebSocket delivery failures during progress streaming can be diagnosed.

findings.summary: WebFetchTool returns errors as JSON-encoded objects while all other tools return plain error strings, breaking the uniform tool error format.
evidence: web.py line 128: WebFetchTool returns json.dumps({'error': '...', 'url': url}) for URL validation failure
web.py line 160: returns json.dumps({'error': str(e), 'url': url}) for fetch exceptions
web.py WebSearchTool line 99: returns f'Error: {e}' — plain string format matching all other tools
filesystem.py, shell.py, network.py, paradise.py, cron.py all return plain 'Error: ...' strings
registry.py line 51 checks result.startswith('Error') which will NOT match JSON-encoded error objects from WebFetchTool
findings.suggestion: Change WebFetchTool's error returns to use the same plain string format as all other tools: return f'Error fetching {url}: {error_msg}'. If structured error data is needed, return the error string for the LLM and log the structured details via logger.
